title: Opening the Joint Interview in Albany
summary: Hasan Kwame Jeffries opens the Civil Rights History Project interview on March 9, 2013, in Albany, Georgia, with Lucius Holloway Sr. and Emma Kate Holloway, and Mr. Holloway gives his birth in Terrell County, Georgia in 1932 to sharecropper parents.

title: Growing Up Sharecropping in Terrell County
summary: Lucius Holloway describes a childhood of sharecropping where the landlord took out the bills and nothing was ever left, yet he remembers it as a good life with food, rest, and loving parents, in a county where cotton and farming were embedded in everything.

title: Enlisting to Help Support the Family
summary: Holloway recounts entering military service in June 1952 as a way to help support his large family, drawing sixty dollars a month as a private and sending an allotment home to his mother.

title: Meeting His Future Wife in 1955
summary: He tells how, only days after his discharge in June 1955, he met Emma Kate for the first time, a woman he had never seen or heard of before that day.

title: The Best Cotton Picker in the Patch
summary: Emma Kate Holloway recalls being a high-school junior in Terrell County who worked at Richmond's Cafe on Main Street and picked four hundred pounds of cotton a day, prouder than her brothers to be her father's best cotton picker.

title: Moving to Dawson and Buying a House
summary: She describes the family's move to Dawson, Georgia, her father farming rented land for a white landlord, and how after a profitable year the landlord took most of the crop, leaving the family to buy a small house with what little remained while her parents took public work in town.

title: A Ride Offered on the Walk Home
summary: Working a job to buy school items, Emma Kate met the young military man Lucius when he rolled up beside her in his car and offered a ride, which she refused because her mother had warned her against riding with boys, especially military boys.

title: Nine O'Clock, a Proposal, and a Family
summary: She recounts Lucius visiting under her mother's strict nine o'clock curfew, his proposal while she was in the twelfth grade, her condition that she still attend school, and their marriage in June 1957 followed by four children, a daughter, Lucius Jr., Charlene, and Patrick in 1964.

part: The Movement and Its Cost at Home
title: A Beautician's Trade and a Lost Job
summary: Emma Kate trained in cosmetology at Albany Tech and opened a hair business her husband built for her, but explains that he was fired from the post office for his SCLC voter-registration work, which she frames as a fight so Black people would not be kept down.

part: The Movement and Its Cost at Home
title: Why He Worked So Hard for the Vote
summary: She explains that Lucius pressed the registration work so Black people could better themselves and escape the cotton and corn patches, even as her own customers grew scarce because white people warned them not to fool around with the Holloways.

part: The Movement and Its Cost at Home
title: Reprisals at Home: The Gas and the Job
summary: Emma Kate recounts a bitter winter around 1959 with a newborn on her lap when someone cut the gas line and drained the tank, forcing the family to borrow a kerosene heater and then a wood heater, and describes how the post office took her husband's job over a pretext about his work hours.

part: The Night They Expected to Die
title: A Warning From the Cook
summary: Emma Kate tells of a night she felt certain they would die, when a Black woman who cooked for white people in the community warned that the whites planned to kill Lucius Holloway that night for his registration work, and begged him not to go to the meeting.

part: The Night They Expected to Die
title: Taking the Children to the Meeting
summary: She recalls Lucius insisting he had to go that night and bringing his whole family along, the four children seated beside her in the church, while a young man outside warned her not to sit by the window because they meant to kill that night.

part: The Night They Expected to Die
title: The Swapped Car and the Ambush
summary: A friend who had been in the military traded cars with Lucius at the meeting so the family could leave a safe way, and when that friend drove the Holloway car back toward Dawson by the creek, armed men waiting to kill Lucius shone flashlights in his face and let him pass.

part: The Night They Expected to Die
title: The Long Way Home, the Killer Spared
summary: Emma Kate describes the family taking a roundabout route that turned a thirty-minute drive into roughly three hours, then tells of a man who came to the house a week or two later to kill Lucius, knelt at the door while the family stayed silent in the dark, and later said he simply could not do it and left town.

title: First Vice President Since 1955
summary: Lucius picks the story back up, recalling Emma Kate's marriage ultimatum and how the Terrell County NAACP appointed him first vice president in 1955, a position he has held ever since through presidents who were beaten, died, or were killed in office.

title: Lawsuits and Black Officeholders
summary: He describes staying on course about the vote despite threats and even Black neighbors avoiding him out of fear, and how filing lawsuits in Terrell County got Black people elected and appointed across nearly every office, including a long-serving Black mayor, his own four years on the City Council, and more than seventeen years on the Board of County Commissioners.

title: Living to See the Change
summary: Holloway reflects that he wanted from boyhood to help people and that the military showed him people living better elsewhere, and expresses amazement and gratitude at living to see the changes he struggled for, citing a newspaper article that named him the last man standing among four local organizers, two of whom were killed.

title: Writing the Book of His Life
summary: He mentions writing a book titled Civil Rights Through the Eyes of Lucius Holloway that gathers much of what they discussed, then is asked the closing question about what he wants younger generations to know about the movement years.

title: A Message to the Younger Generation
summary: Lucius urges young people to trust God and stay focused on what they believe in, and Emma Kate adds that they can make a difference by respecting their elders and themselves, getting a good education, and staying in school, before the interview closes.
